title: LatinDiab
text:
The LatinDiab project ambition is to strengthening health systems research capacity and decision making in diabetes care by contributing with a well documented set of open-source tools for the setup of modern, scalable, and efficient diabetes registers in Latin America, ensuring high quality, reproducible, and transparent research leading to improve quality of diabetes care, increase the scientific impact of these resources, and guide decision making in diabetes care and planning. Ultimately, LatinDiab will provide a proof of concept regarding how continuously updated and automated health data could be linked in order to establish a disease register. Thus, the scope of the open-source software could be transferable to other non-communicable chronic diseases and settings.
The overall aim of the LatinDiab project is to co-design, implement, and evaluate an open-source software that will be integrated into existing health information systems for the set-up of diabetes registers in Mexico, Argentina, and Colombia. Specifically, this project aims to

Map the available data infrastructure from a data life cycle perspective: planning, generation, collection, processing, storing, management, access, content, permissions, maintenance; overall data flows and potential linkage, the relevant staff working with data at any stage of the data life cycle.
Engage key staff involved at any point of the data life cycle to co-design context specific implementation strategies of the open-source software.
Deploy, adapt, and integrate into current health information systems an open-source software to build a diabetes register.
Build research capacity in open science practices and reproducible workflows through online and onsite training sessions.
Co-develop the road map for scaling-up the implementation of the open-source software and the implementation of a federated meta-analysis.

title: Work package 5
text:
The current Colombian health system remains fragmented, though it is in the process of transitioning towards a universal and equitable coverage model for the entire territory. In this context, we propose to implement a monitoring model based on health records and other health-related data sources from the city of Medellín (Figure 3) a city with approximately 2.63 million residents—and the broader Aburrá Valley, which is home to around 4.25 million inhabitants.
This WP will apply the capture–recapture method to estimate both the incidence and prevalence of type 1 diabetes (T1D) in Medellín and the Aburrá Valley. The approach requires compiling a census of all T1D cases recorded in each data-generating center (e.g., hospitals, outpatient clinics, laboratories, pharmacies), even when the data sources are incomplete. Since each center provides only a fragment of the total cases, the capture–recapture method allows estimation of the total number of cases by combining these partial records. Using statistical models, it becomes possible to generate more accurate estimates of the epidemiological indicators of interest. Figure 3B summarizes the information sources, datasets, variables, and definitions that will be used to build the T1D registry.
The population denominator will be defined based on official projections derived from the 2018 Colombian National Population and Housing Census, adjusted for the study period. Because individuals with T1D are widely dispersed across the health system and there is no centralized or interoperable registry for this condition in Medellín, the Aburrá Valley, or (to our knowledge) anywhere else in Latin America, we will use a non-probabilistic convenience sampling strategy to select data-generating centers. To improve accuracy, cases will be cross-referenced across different sources to identify overlaps. The primary identifier will be the patient’s national identity number, while the secondary identifiers will include first name, last name, and date of birth. For privacy and confidentiality, each individual will then be assigned a unique internal identification code, which will allow anonymization of the databases while facilitating linkage across multiple sources.
Incidence will be calculated as the number of newly diagnosed T1D cases identified during the study period, divided by the total population at risk in Medellín and the Aburrá Valley. Prevalence will be calculated as the total number of existing T1D cases (new and pre-existing) during the same period, divided by the total population of the area. Mortality will be estimated as the number of deaths attributed to T1D during the study period, divided by the total number of individuals with T1D in the population.
By using different health information sources, WP5 aim to implement a population-based T1D register with the purpose to estimate the incidence and prevalence of diagnosed T1D and the related complications.

title: Work package 3
text:
Argentina has a population of around 45 million people living in 23 provinces and the autonomous city of Buenos Aires (Figure 2). The province of La Pampa has 361,859 inhabitants living in an area of 143,440 km² (2022 Census). The public health system in the province of La Pampa consists of 126 healthcare facilities located across 80 localities, with about 11,000 authorized users, including health professionals and administrative staff. This health care network is administratively organized into five health zones.
The Health Information System (SIS, from its acronym in Spanish) provides an online appointment system and a central appointment desk for referral to secondary and tertiary levels of care. This information system is crucial for data access and evaluating the impact of health policies. However, the full capacity of the SIS for the diabetes programme management and decision making has not been exploited yet. Local challenges include the lack of the technical skills to perform the data linkage and analyzing the data.
According to the 4th National Survey of Risk Factors (INDEC, 2019), the prevalence of diabetes in La Pampa province was estimated at 12.7% in 2019. Approximately 17,000 patients are receiving diabetes care within the public health system, with 4,300 of these being insulin-treated (Provincial Healthcare IT System). “La Pampa Diabetes Programme” was established in the same year under the management of the Evita General Community Hospital with the objectives to improve access to care, ensure timely referrals and appropriate treatment, and implement a comprehensive approach to reduce diabetes-related complications and enhance the quality of life for patients.
WP3 will be coordinated with health research entities in La Pampa province, including the Research Area of the Ministry of Health, La Pampa Provincial Council for Health Research, and the National University of La Pampa. WP3 has the following objectives:

To map and identify all potential health data sources within the health information systems that could be potentially used to identify people living with diabetes in La Pampa province.
To establish a register-based definition of diabetes using the available proxy indicators in the health data sources within the health information systems of La Pampa province.
To integrate a diabetes provincial register through linkage of different health data sources.
To analyze the diabetes provincial register to provide insights about the epidemiology of diabetes in La Pampa province.
To describe diabetes care using the provincial diabetes register in La Pampa province.

title: Work package 1
text:
This WP has three objectives, 1) to lead the overall management of the project, 2) to coordinate the collaboration between WPs and 3) ensure the transfer of technical skills for the implementation of the open-source software, and the implementation of a high-quality technical documentation of the project following open science and reproducibility principles.
WP1 will implement the Seedcase Project, an open-source data infrastructure tool that is compliant with the FAIR principles (Findable, Accessible, Interoperable, Reusable). It is designed to support modern, scalable, and efficient approaches to health information systems [REF-seedcase webpage]. By using state-of-the-art software engineering practices, Seedcase project ensures that health data are FAIR, meeting a robust stewardship, open and reproducible science. This will enable the integration of high quality, standardized registry data from Argentina, Mexico and Colombia into a shared framework. By documenting the full data life cycle, from generation to analysis and interpretation, Seedcase will deliver transparent, reproducible and policy-relevant data resources for research, health system planning, and decision making within diabetes care [REF sprout webpage].
WP1 will deploy the technical infrastructure and build local technical capacity to maintain and expand these systems, prioritizing sustainability (maintainability over time), capacity building and equity in digital health. The emphasis on co-design and open documentation ensures that the implementation of Seedcase is context-specific, scalable, and potentially transferable to other non-communicable diseases. This approach strengthens health system resilience, foster collaboration across countries, and provides a blueprint for future cooperation in digital health innovation. Figure 1 provides a graphical summary of the Seedcase-Sprout package.
[Figure 1.](figure1-latindiab.png)
Usage of this software tool depends on the quality of its documentation and training material, for WP2-WP4 there will be a tech lead who will liaise between WP1 training activities and local data and research practices. Two key elements in WP1 are the research operations that will simplify, streamline, and automate the collaborations and coordination of activities and products between WPs; and the technical and research capacity building that will provide the learning environment of the required technical skills in the use of modern data engineering tools through formal and informal training sessions, creating and running short workshops, and tutorials that will teach the LatinDiab members how they can use this software tool, and local data engineers on how to implement it.

title: Work package 2
text:
WP2 will use two methods of implementation science, co-creation and implementation strategies. In implementation science, two questions are key to be answered: the “what innovation will be implemented?” and the “how will the innovation be implemented?”,. The “what?” in the LatinDiab-1 project, is a software tool  for streamlining and automating the data ingestion, processing, storage and management to process continuously and automatically collected data .  The “how?”  are the different strategies to engage those key players that will  implement the software, or are involved in the data life cycle, and those who will use the output data. 
The LatinDiab-1 project will co-create the open source software, and the implementation strategies with stakeholders, all the relevant players involved in the data full data cycle and the end users. It is of most importance to understand their perspective, experience, needs, and previously used tools, in order to successfully implement a context-specific high-value tool.
Objectives: 
WP2 has two main objectives: (i) To explore stakeholders’ perspectives, people involved in the health data full data cycle, and users of current health data repositories, about the current health information systems and their willingness to adopt modern software tools to establish a type 1 diabetes register . (ii) Co-create the implementation strategies  for the set up of type 1 diabetes register  in different contexts from LatAm.
Description of work: 
WP2 will use qualitative methods, semi-structured interviews and participatory methods to achieve its main objectives. 
Participants: Stakeholders and staff involved in the full data cycle from the macro, meso and micro level. Macro level such as national decision makers, and academics, meso level including decision makers at the regional level as well as people responsible of the health information systems at the regional level as well as reference hospitals (statistics department, epidemiology,  among others) and at the micro level such as healthcare workers, statistics department, end users at the health care network, organizations and patients. 
(i) Semi-structure interviews will be applied to stakeholders from the macro, meso and micro level.  The interview guide will be developed using the FAIR guiding principles and the Rapid Assessment Protocol for Insulin Access (RAPIA) methodology that includes 11 themes to evaluate how the health system responds to people with type 1 diabetes. The study will include 25 interviews in each country, these will be transcribed verbatim, and analyzed using the high-quality health system framework [REF].  
(ii) Co-creation workshops will be conducted with stakeholders, key staff players involved in the data full data cycle, and end users. The aim is to co-create the T1D register and the implementation strategy. Three workshops with the participation of stakeholders and relevant players involved in the full data cycle, and the end user from the macro, meso and micro level, ideally altogether, if not possible, separate meetings will be organized.
During the first workshop the information collected with the semi-structure interviews will be shared, and initial ideas about the software tool. During the second workshop the first version of the digital platform will be shared to collect information about the usability of the system as well as the implementation strategy will be prioritized and decided with them and a final workshop will be held to test the digital platform and collect the final changes to be implemented. The information collected will be used to adapt the digital platform to each of the three contexts.

title: Work package 4
text:
Established in 1943, the Mexican Institute for Social Security (IMSS by its acronym in Spanish) is Mexico’s largest public health institution, responsible for delivering medical care, social security benefits, and pension services to formally employed individuals, retirees, and their dependents such as spouses, children, and in some cases, parents and other dependents, who are legally recognized under IMSS regulations. By the end of 2024, IMSS served a total of 77,430,917 beneficiaries which represent 58.5% of the Mexican population (IMSS, 2025). 
Between 2000 and 2018, institutional health data from the IMSS revealed fluctuating but concerning trends in the incidence of T1D among children and adolescents under 19 years of age. Initial increases from 3.4 to 6.2 cases per 100,000 insured individuals between 2000 and 2006 were followed by variable peaks and declines (Gómez-Díaz RA et al. 2012; Wacher NH et al. 2020). The overall incidence slightly declined to 2.83 by 2018, age-specific and sex-specific analyses showed persistent growth in older pediatric groups (10–19 years), with adolescent females consistently exhibiting higher rates than males (Wacher NH et al. 2020). People living with T1D affiliated to IMSS face a complex set of clinical and social challenges that demand urgent attention. Most people showed poor glycemic control, had a high prevalence of complications (microvascular in 64.1% of cases) and frequent episodes of hypoglycemia and ketoacidosis, which led to high rates of emergency room visits (39%) and hospitalizations (33.9%). 
A series of databases from IMSS’ Health Information System comprises a comprehensive set of components and modules that document healthcare delivery processes. The system is designed to generate and disseminate statistical information that supports institutional decision-making. Data includes information on morbidity, mortality, births, population demographics, health services coverage, and the availability of human, infrastructural, material, and financial resources. We will use these datasets to implement the open-source software tool for the set-up of the diabetes register.
The absence of comprehensive registries for T1D impedes outcome monitoring, intervention evaluation, and equitable access to specialized care. Addressing these gaps through targeted research and implementation efforts is essential to reduce long-term complications and improve health outcomes among IMSS patients. Thus, a T1D registry would be a strategic asset for IMSS, enabling real-time tracking of clinical outcomes, identification of high-risk individuals, and monitoring of treatment adherence. By integrating sociodemographic and clinical data, IMSS could design targeted interventions, optimize resource allocation, and assess program effectiveness. Furthermore, the registry could inform policy decisions related to disability benefits, preventive care, and endocrinology service planning, shifting the focus from reactive crisis management to proactive, long-term health strategies and cost containment.
WP4 will be implemented within the IMSS to explore how routinely collected clinical and administrative data can be leveraged to standardize and enhance epidemiologic monitoring, and decision making. WP4 seeks to demonstrate how strategic data use within IMSS can serve as a scalable model for improving chronic disease management and advancing health equity across Mexico.
